this same effort of Luther's:


"The long columns of print and
the big pictures of bottles, dives
and sordid dens are all devoted,
with an air of judicial detachment,
to showing that AN impartial in
vestigation proves that prohibition
is not a success Who paid the
expenses of the impartial investi-
CATION? Whose money paid for
setting the type and making the
plates? Can it be that the harried
brewers are initiating Standard Oil?
Careless and indolent editors there
are who thoughtlessly use such
stuff; others there are to whom ap-
peals the saving OF the expense of
setting a page of type; and all Of
them serve the interest of whoever
is back of this philanthropic enter
prise" -...


No reputable newspaper associa-
ton would handle such stuff as
news nor would a newspaper work
ing on the principle of giving it's
readers exactly what it pretends to
give them furnish space for such
stuff. An artistically dressed adj
vertisement of this sort slipped in
as news is able to accomplish for
the interests 1t favors what thou
sands of dollars worth of space
purchased outright could not ac-
complish And the reading public
is buncoed accordingly. Mr.. Luther
evidently has an opportunity for
steady employment at high prices
along his special line since there
are some papers in the country
who are willing to aid his enter
priSn
